title: Okay, this unleashed my agent
summary: AI Jason reviews features of agent-based AI systems including autoresearch, memory architectures, and self-evolving mechanisms. The video covers multiple agent frameworks and capabilities that enable autonomous task execution and skill development.
writeup: This video surveys the current state of self-evolving agents by drawing a hard line between two distinct approaches: harness-improvement loops (auto agent, auto research) that rewrite the agent software itself via a program.md vision, evaluation suite, and keep-or-discard loop, versus in-context learning systems (Claude Code, OpenHands, Hermes Agent) that accumulate facts, skills, and session history across runs. The through-line is a three-pillar memory architecture, hot facts always in the system prompt, warm facts loaded on demand, skills as domain knowledge, and history as a searchable conversation log, and the core argument is that purely prompt-based memory fails because LLMs reliably forget to write updates, making a background consolidation step necessary.
key_points: Auto agent and auto research improve the agent harness itself by running a loop: define a vision in program.md, let Claude Code or Codex rewrite the runtime or scripts, run an evaluation suite, keep improvements that win and discard the rest; the output is a frozen harness, not a continuously learning agent.

In-context self-learning systems organize memory into three pillars: facts (hot memory always in the system prompt, warm memory loaded on demand), skills (domain knowledge for specific task types), and history (raw conversation logs the agent can search); Claude Code, OpenHands, and Hermes Agent each implement these pillars differently.

Claude Code evolved from a single CLAUDE.md file through a hot-or-warm memory convention that most users already practice, to an auto-memory feature that writes typed files into .claude/memory/ indexed by memory.md, to a background auto-trim process discovered in the leaked source code that consolidates and prunes stale entries after a session ends.

The fundamental weakness of Claude Code's memory system is that it is purely prompt-based: the model must remember to create and update memory files, and when it forgets, stale information accumulates in the context and degrades performance.

OpenHands (OpenClaw) treats memory as a first-class citizen with a richer set of predefined memory file types and a bootstrap.md that instructs the agent to proactively gather information from users, filling the skill and searchable history gaps that Claude Code leaves open.
